suggested.title: [Read Aloud] Stories with characters who disagree
[Read Aloud] The True Story of the Three Little Pigs
suggested.author: Jon Scieszka

competence_markers.description: The student can identify straw man arguments in conversations and media
The student can present a tradition they do not share at its strongest — fairly enough that a practitioner would recognize it
The student can narrate back another person's position before responding to it
The student can tell a story fairly from both sides before offering their own assessment
The student can articulate why an intelligent person might hold a belief the student considers wrong
The student has attempted to steel-man the opposite of one of their own beliefs
The student treats disagreement as an opportunity to understand, not as a battle to win

narration_prompts.prompt: What is a straw man? Give me an example.
narration_prompts.detail: Then show me what the steel man version would be.

narration_prompts.prompt: Pick a tradition from 1.3 that you do not personally believe in. Present it at its strongest
narration_prompts.detail: — as if you were a friendly believer explaining it to someone who has never heard of it.

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a disagreement where both sides might be right about something.
narration_prompts.detail: What is each side responding to? What does each side see that the other misses?

narration_prompts.prompt: Take a story you know and tell it fairly from both sides.
narration_prompts.detail: Then tell me which version you heard first and how it was different.

narration_prompts.prompt: Why would an intelligent person believe something you think is wrong?
narration_prompts.detail: Pick a specific belief and explain what genuine need it addresses.

narration_prompts.prompt: Try to make the best argument against one of your own beliefs.
narration_prompts.detail: What would someone who disagrees with you say? What is the strongest version of their argument?

cross_connections.description: Political disagreements are worldview disagreements. Steel-manning is essential to understanding political conflict.
The narration skills practiced throughout 1.3 are directly applied here.
Intellectual honesty and the courage to understand people you disagree with are character virtues.

content.why_this_matters:
In every other packet in 1.3, you have learned about what different people believe. You have met the gods of many traditions. You have learned about ideas that may be very different from what you believe. Some of those ideas might have surprised you. Some might have seemed strange. Some might have seemed beautiful. Some might have seemed wrong.

This packet teaches you the most important skill in all of 1.3: **how to listen to someone you disagree with, understand what they actually believe, and say it back to them fairly — before you tell them why you think they are wrong.**

This sounds simple. It is actually one of the hardest things in the world.

Most people do not listen to understand. They listen to respond. While the other person is talking, they are already thinking about what they will say back. They are looking for the weakest part of the argument so they can attack it. They are building a version of the other person's belief that is easy to knock down.

That is called a **straw man** — making the other person's argument weaker than it actually is so you can beat it more easily. It is intellectually dishonest, and it means you never actually understood what the other person was saying.

The opposite is a **steel man** — making the other person's argument as strong as possible, even if you disagree with it. Finding the best version of what they believe. Understanding why an intelligent, thoughtful person would believe it. Only then — after you have truly understood it — do you explain why you think it is wrong.

This is not about being nice (though it is nice). It is about being honest. A person who can only beat the weak version of an argument they disagree with has not actually beaten the argument. They have beaten a version of it that nobody serious actually holds.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **The habit of listening before responding** — truly hearing what someone says before preparing a reply
2. **The ability to narrate someone else's position accurately** — saying back what they actually believe, not what you think they believe
3. **Fairness in storytelling** — telling a story that is fair to all sides
4. **The recognition that people who disagree can both be honest** — disagreement does not mean someone is stupid or evil
5. **The concepts of straw man and steel man** — at an age-appropriate level
6. **The practice of charitable interpretation** — assuming the best about what someone means until proven otherwise
content.core_content:
### Part 1: The Straw Man — What Not to Do

Imagine your friend says: "I think chocolate ice cream is the best flavor."

A straw man response would be: "So you think all other flavors are disgusting and nobody should ever eat them? That's ridiculous!"

Your friend did not say that. They said chocolate is the best. They did not say other flavors are disgusting. They did not say nobody should eat anything else. You took their argument, made it sound silly, and then attacked the silly version.

This is what a straw man is. It is taking what someone actually said and changing it — making it weaker, more extreme, or more ridiculous — so you can argue against the changed version instead of the real one.

People do this all the time, and most of them do not realize they are doing it.

### Part 2: The Steel Man — What to Do Instead

Now imagine your friend says: "I think chocolate ice cream is the best flavor."

A steel man response would be: "Okay, so you think chocolate is the best because it's rich and complex — it can be dark and intense or sweet and creamy. It goes well with lots of other things. And most people agree it's one of the most popular flavors. I can see why you'd say that."

Then — and only then — you might say: "I actually prefer vanilla, because I think it has a clean, pure flavor that goes with everything, and I think chocolate can sometimes be too heavy."

Do you see the difference? In the steel man version, you made your friend's argument as strong as you could before you disagreed. You showed them that you understood what they meant. And your disagreement was with their actual argument, not with a weaker version of it.

### Part 3: Why Does This Matter Beyond Ice Cream?

Now apply this to the things you have learned in 1.3.

Imagine someone says: "I believe in the Norse gods. I think Odin's sacrifice for wisdom shows the highest value any tradition has ever named — that knowledge is worth any price."

A straw man: "You believe in fairy tales? Thor and Loki are just comic book characters."

A steel man: "So you believe that the Norse tradition's central insight — that wisdom requires sacrifice, and that even the gods must pay for knowledge — is genuinely profound. You think this tells us something real about the human condition that other traditions miss. I can see why that would be meaningful."

The steel man does not mean you agree. It means you understand. And understanding is the prerequisite for honest disagreement.

### Part 4: The Narration Practice

In this curriculum, you have been practicing narration — telling back what you learned in your own words. Steel-manning is narration applied to beliefs you may not share.

The practice is simple: after learning about any tradition's beliefs, narrate them back as if you were a friendly, intelligent believer explaining your own faith to someone who has never heard of it. Not defending it. Not attacking it. Just explaining it — at its best, in its strongest form.

"The Yoruba believe in one supreme God, Olodumare, who is too vast to approach directly. His power reaches humans through the orishas — divine forces in nature that you can have a real, personal relationship with. It's not that there are many gods — it's that the one God has many faces."

That is a steel man. It presents the belief in its best light, the way a thoughtful Yoruba practitioner would want it presented.

### Part 5: People Who Disagree Can Both Be Right About Something

Here is a hard idea: when two people disagree, it is possible that they are both honest, both intelligent, and both right about something.

A Christian who says "I believe God created the world with purpose and meaning" is responding to a real human need — the need to believe life has meaning. This is not a silly belief. It addresses something deep.

A secular humanist who says "I believe humans can create their own meaning through love, work, and discovery" is also responding to a real human need — the need for autonomy and self-determination. This is not a silly belief either.

They disagree. They might both be wrong. But they are both addressing a real question — "What is life for?" — and their answers are both worth taking seriously.

The person who dismisses either one without understanding it has not thought hard enough.

### Part 6: Fairness in Storytelling

When you tell a story about a disagreement — between two friends, between two countries, between two religions — fairness means telling both sides at their best.

Imagine telling the story of the Crusades. An unfair version from the Christian side: "Christians went to free the Holy Land from evil Muslim invaders." An unfair version from the Muslim side: "Christians invaded Muslim lands out of greed and fanaticism."

A fair version: "Christians believed the Holy Land was sacred and that they had a duty to reclaim it. Muslims had controlled the region for centuries and considered Christian military campaigns to be invasions of their homeland. Both sides included people who were sincere believers and people who were motivated by power and wealth. The violence was terrible. The question of who was 'right' is one of the hardest in history."

Fairness does not mean both sides are equally right. It means both sides are presented honestly — at their best and at their worst — before you decide.

### Part 7: Practicing with Real Disagreements

The best way to develop this skill is to practice it every day.

When you disagree with a sibling: before you argue, say back what they actually want and why. "You want to watch your show because you've been waiting all day and it really matters to you. I get that. And I want to watch mine because..."

When you hear about a belief that seems strange: before you say "that's weird," try to understand why an intelligent person would believe it. What problem does it solve? What need does it meet? What would the world look like from inside that belief?

When you hear adults arguing about politics, religion, or anything else: mentally practice steel-manning both sides. Can you state each side more fairly than the person arguing for it?

This practice is the foundation of everything in 1.3.9 — and of everything else in this curriculum that requires you to think honestly about people who see the world differently than you do.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: The Straw Man Hunt [Early Packet]
Listen for straw man arguments in everyday life — in conversations, in media, in stories. When the student hears one, they identify it: "That's a straw man because the person actually said ___, not ___."

### Study 2: Steel-Manning a Tradition [Ongoing]
After every 1.3 packet, the student practices: present this tradition at its strongest, as if you were a friendly, intelligent believer. The mentor evaluates: would a real practitioner recognize this as fair?

### Study 3: The Two-Sided Story [Mid-Packet]
Take a historical disagreement (the Crusades, the colonization of the Americas, or any conflict from 1.1). The student tells both sides fairly before offering their own assessment.

### Study 4: The Disagreement Practice [Ongoing]
During real family disagreements, the student practices: "Before I tell you what I think, let me say back what you think." This becomes a family habit.

### Study 5: Why Would a Smart Person Believe This? [Mid-to-Late Packet]
Take a belief the student finds strange or wrong. The student answers: Why would an intelligent, thoughtful person believe this? What problem does it solve? What need does it meet?

### Study 6: The Fair Version [Late Packet]
Take a story the student has been told one way (by a book, a teacher, a parent) and ask: What would the other side say? Is there a fairer version of this story?

### Study 7: Self-Examination [End of Packet]
The student picks one of their own beliefs and tries to steel-man the opposite position. Can they make the case against their own belief as strongly as possible?

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Straw Man Identification
**Developing well:** The student spontaneously identifies straw man arguments in conversations, stories, and media.
**Developing slowly:** Cannot distinguish between a fair representation and a distortion.

### 2. Fair Narration
**Developing well:** When asked to present a belief they do not share, the student does so fairly enough that a practitioner would nod along.
**Developing slowly:** Presentation is tinged with mockery, dismissal, or obvious unfairness.

### 3. The Listening Habit
**Developing well:** The student has begun to listen before responding — narrating back the other person's position before stating their own.
**Developing slowly:** The student listens only to find ammunition for a response.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Treating This as "Being Nice"
Steel-manning is not about niceness. It is about intellectual honesty. A student who steel-mans because they want to avoid conflict has missed the point. The goal is understanding, which leads to stronger disagreement, not weaker.

### Demanding the Student Abandon Their Own Position
Some mentors worry that steel-manning will make the student relativist — unable to hold their own beliefs. The opposite is true. The student who can represent the strongest version of a position they disagree with and still disagree has the strongest possible basis for their own position.
